problem:
Exercise 1.1
Let $k\in\mathbf{N}$, let $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbf{R}$ with $\alpha\leq\beta$, and for each coefficient vector $a=(a_1,\dots,a_k)\in\mathbf{R}^k$ define the polynomial

\[
p(t)=a_1+a_2 t+\cdots+a_k t^{k-1}, \qquad t\in\mathbf{R}.
\]

Is the set

\[
\{a\in\mathbf{R}^k\mid p(0)=1,\ |p(t)|\leq 1\text{ for all } t\in[\alpha,\beta]\}
\]

convex?

Exercise 1.2
\emph{Set distributive characterization of convexity} [Rockafellar]. Let $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$. Show that $C$ is convex if and only if
$$(\alpha + \beta)C = \alpha C + \beta C$$
for all nonnegative scalars $\alpha, \beta$, where
$$\alpha C = \{\alpha x \mid x \in C\}, \qquad \alpha C + \beta C = \{u+v \mid u \in \alpha C,\ v \in \beta C\}.$$

Exercise 1.3
\emph{Composition of linear-fractional functions.} Let $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$, $c \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $d \in \mathbf{R}$, and let $E \in \mathbf{R}^{p\times m}$, $f \in \mathbf{R}^p$, $g \in \mathbf{R}^m$, $h \in \mathbf{R}$. Suppose $\phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m$ and $\psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p$ are the linear-fractional functions

\[
\phi(x) = \frac{Ax + b}{c^T x + d}, \qquad
\psi(y) = \frac{Ey + f}{g^T y + h},
\]

with domains
\[
\mathbf{dom}\,\phi = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\}, \qquad
\mathbf{dom}\,\psi = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^m \mid g^T y + h > 0\}.
\]
We associate with $\phi$ and $\psi$ the matrices

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \\
c^T & d
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \\
g^T & h
\end{bmatrix},
\]

respectively.

Now consider the composition $\Gamma = \psi \circ \phi$, i.e., $\Gamma(x) = \psi(\phi(x))$, with domain

\[
\mathbf{dom}\,\Gamma = \{x \in \mathbf{dom}\,\phi \mid \phi(x) \in \mathbf{dom}\,\psi\}.
\]

Show that $\Gamma$ is linear-fractional, and that the matrix associated with it is the product

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \\
g^T & h
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \\
c^T & d
\end{bmatrix}.
\]

Exercise 1.4
Dual of exponential cone. Consider the exponential cone $K_{\exp} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^3$ defined by

\[
K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid y > 0,\ ye^{x/y} \leq z\}.
\]

Find its dual cone
\[
K_{\exp}^* = \{u \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid u^T v \geq 0\ \text{for all } v \in K_{\exp}\}.
\]

We are not worried here about the fine details of what happens on the boundaries of these cones, so you really needn't worry about it. But we make some comments here for those who do care about such things.

The cone $K_{\exp}$ as defined above is not closed. To obtain its closure, we need to add the points

\[
\{(x,y,z) \mid x \leq 0,\ y = 0,\ z \geq 0\}.
\]

(This makes no difference, since the dual of a cone is equal to the dual of its closure.)

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. For a cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, its dual cone is defined by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(a) Show that $C \cap D$ and $C^* + D^*$ are convex cones. (In fact, $C \cap D$ and $C^* + D^*$ are closed, but we won't ask you to show this.)

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$, and let the dual cone of a cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be defined by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(b) Show that $(C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*$.

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$, and for a cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(c) Now show that $(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^*$. You can do this by first showing

\[
(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \Longleftrightarrow C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^* .
\]

You can use the following result:

\[
\text{If } K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n \text{ is a closed convex cone, then } K^{**} = K.
\]

Next, show that $C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*$ and conclude that $(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*$.

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(d) Let $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$, and define the polyhedral cone

\[
V = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid Ax \succeq 0\},
\]

where $Ax \succeq 0$ means that each component of $Ax$ is nonnegative. Show that the dual cone of $V$ can be expressed as

\[
V^* = \{A^T v \mid v \succeq 0\},
\]

where $v \in \mathbf{R}^m$ and $v \succeq 0$ means that each component of $v$ is nonnegative.

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. Let $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be any set. Its polar is defined by

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(a) Show that $C^\circ$ is convex, even if $C$ itself is not convex.

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of a set $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(b) Suppose $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is a cone, i.e., $\theta x \in C$ for all $x \in C$ and all $\theta \ge 0$. What is the polar of $C$?

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as the set

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(c) Let $\|\cdot\|$ be a norm on $\mathbf{R}^n$, and let its unit ball be
\[
B = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \|x\| \leq 1\}.
\]
What is the polar set $B^\circ$ of this unit ball?

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. For a set $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, its polar is defined by

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(d) Let $\mathbf{1} \in \mathbf{R}^n$ denote the all-ones vector, and consider the set
\[
C = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\, x \succeq 0\}.
\]
What is the polar set $C^\circ$?

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. Let $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be a set. Its polar is defined by

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(e) Show that if $C$ is closed and convex and satisfies $0 \in C$, then
\[
(C^\circ)^\circ = C.
\]

Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For each cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$, describe its dual cone
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]

(a) $K = \{0\}$.

Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For a cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$, its dual cone is defined by
$$K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.$$ Describe the dual cone for the following cone:

(b) $K = \mathbf{R}^2$.

Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For the cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$, describe its dual cone
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]

(c) $K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid |x_1| \leq x_2\}$.

Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For the cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$, describe its dual cone
$$
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
$$

(d) $K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\}$.

Exercise 2.1
Maximum of a convex function over a polyhedron. Let $v_1,\ldots,v_k\in\mathbb{R}^n$, let
\[
P=\operatorname{conv}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n,
\]
and let $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ be a convex function. Show that the maximum of $f$ over the polyhedron $P$ is achieved at one of its vertices, \emph{i.e.},

\[
\sup_{x\in P} f(x)=\max_{i=1,\ldots,k} f(v_i).
\]

(A stronger statement is: the maximum of a convex function over a closed bounded convex set is achieved at an extreme point, \emph{i.e.}, a point in the set that is not a convex combination of any other points in the set.) \emph{Hint.} Assume the statement is false, and use Jensen's inequality.

Exercise 2.2
\emph{A general vector composition rule.} Let $h:\mathbf{R}^k\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^k$, and let $g_i:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$, $i=1,\dots,k$. Define $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ by

\[
f(x)=h(g_1(x),g_2(x),\ldots,g_k(x)).
\]

Assume that for each $i\in\{1,\dots,k\}$, one of the following holds:

- $h$ is nondecreasing in the $i$th argument (with all other arguments fixed), and $g_i$ is convex;

- $h$ is nonincreasing in the $i$th argument (with all other arguments fixed), and $g_i$ is concave;

- $g_i$ is affine.

Show that $f$ is convex on $\mathbf{R}^n$. (This composition rule subsumes all the ones given in the book, and is the one used in software systems such as CVX. The result also holds in the general case when the monotonicity conditions listed above are imposed on $\tilde h$, the extended-valued extension of $h$.)

Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} Let
\[
f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx),
\]
where $x\in \mathbf{R}^n$, $t\in \mathbf{R}$, and
\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}
\]
(i.e., the interior of the second-order cone). The function $f$ is convex. (The function $f$ is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of $f$ using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(a) Let $u\in \mathbf{R}^n$ and $t\in \mathbf{R}_{++}$, and consider the function
\[
(u,t)\mapsto t-(1/t)u^Tu
\]
on the domain $\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}_{++}\}$. Explain why this function is concave on $\operatorname{dom} f=\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|u\|_2\}$. \emph{Hint.} Use convexity of the quadratic-over-linear function.

Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} Let $f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx)$, with
\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\},
\]
(i.e., the interior of the second-order cone). The function $f$ is convex. In this exercise you establish convexity of $f$ using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(b) Let $u\in \mathbf{R}^n$ and $t\in \mathbf{R}$. Using the result established previously, show that the function
\[
(u,t)\mapsto -\log\!\left(t-\frac{1}{t}u^Tu\right)
\]
is convex on
\[
\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|u\|_2\}=\operatorname{dom} f.
\]

Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} Let
\[
f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx),
\]
where $(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}$ and
\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}.
\]
(Thus $t^2-x^Tx>0$ on the domain.) The function $f$ is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone. In the previous parts, one establishes that
\[
f(x,t)= -\log(t-\|x\|_2)-\log(t+\|x\|_2),
\]
and that this representation can be obtained from standard convexity-preserving composition rules using known convexity of $-\log(\cdot)$ and properties of the functions $t\pm\|x\|_2$ on the domain $t>\|x\|_2$.

(c) Using these facts, show that $f$ is convex on its domain.

Exercise 2.4
\emph{A quadratic-over-linear composition theorem.} Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a convex function satisfying $f(x)\ge 0$ for all $x\in \operatorname{dom} f$, and let $g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a concave function satisfying $g(x)>0$ for all $x\in \operatorname{dom} g$. Show that the function
\[
h(x)=\frac{f(x)^2}{g(x)},
\]
defined on the domain $\operatorname{dom} h=\operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g$, is convex.

Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with domain $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}^n$ and satisfying $f(0)\leq 0$. Define its perspective by
\[
g(x,t)=t f(x/t),
\]
for $(x,t)$ in the domain $\{(x,t)\mid t>0\}$.

(a) Show that for each fixed $x\in \mathbf{R}^n$, the function $t\mapsto t f(x/t)$ is nonincreasing on $t>0$.

Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0)\leq 0$.

(b) Let $g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a concave function, and assume $g(x)>0$ for all $x\in \operatorname{dom} g$. Show that the function

\[
h(x)=g(x)f(x/g(x)), \qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\{x\in \operatorname{dom} g\mid x/g(x)\in \operatorname{dom} f\}
\]

is convex on its domain.

Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0)\leq 0$.

(c) As an example, show that the function $h:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ defined by

\[
h(x)=\frac{x^Tx}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n,
\]

where $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid x_k>0,\ k=1,\dots,n\}$, is convex on $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$.

Exercise 2.6
Perspective of log determinant. Let $X\in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$ be an $n\times n$ real symmetric positive definite matrix, and let $t\in \mathbf{R}_{++}$. Show that
\[
f(X,t)=nt\log t-t\log\det X,
\]
with domain $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\times \mathbf{R}_{++}$, is convex in the pair $(X,t)$.

Use this to show that the function $g:\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\to \mathbf{R}$ defined by
\[
g(X)=n(\operatorname{tr}X)\log(\operatorname{tr}X)-(\operatorname{tr}X)(\log\det X)
\]
that is,
\[
g(X)= n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\left(\log\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i-\sum_{i=1}^n \log\lambda_i\right),
\]
where $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$ are the eigenvalues of $X$, is convex on $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$.

Exercise 2.7
Pre-composition with a linear fractional mapping. Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^m\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, and let $A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b\in\mathbf{R}^m$, $c\in\mathbf{R}^n$, and $d\in\mathbf{R}$. Define $g$ on the set
\[
\operatorname{dom} g=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid c^Tx+d>0\}
\]
by
\[
g(x)=(c^Tx+d)\,f\!\left(\frac{Ax+b}{c^Tx+d}\right),
\]
where $\dfrac{Ax+b}{c^Tx+d}$ denotes division of the vector $Ax+b\in\mathbf{R}^m$ by the positive scalar $c^Tx+d$. Show that $g$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} g$.

Exercise 2.8
Scalar valued linear fractional functions. Let $a,c\in\mathbf{R}^n$ and $b,d\in\mathbf{R}$, and consider the function $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ defined by
$$
f(x)=\frac{a^T x+b}{c^T x+d},
$$
with domain
$$
\operatorname{dom} f=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid c^T x+d>0\},
$$
assumed nonempty. Such a function is called linear fractional. Determine when a linear fractional function is convex, and when a linear fractional function is quasiconvex.

Exercise 2.9
Let $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ and $b\in\mathbb{R}^m$. Show that the function

\[
f(x)=\frac{\|Ax-b\|_2^2}{1-x^Tx}, \qquad x\in\mathbb{R}^n,
\]

is convex on the open unit ball $\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n\mid \|x\|_2<1\}$.

Exercise 2.10
Weighted geometric mean. Let $n\in\mathbf{N}$, and consider the function $f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ defined by

\[
f(x)=\prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k},
\]

where the weights $\alpha_k\geq 0$ satisfy $\sum_{k=1}^n \alpha_k\leq 1$. The geometric mean $f(x)=\left(\prod_k x_k\right)^{1/n}$ with $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$ is concave, as shown on page 74. Extend that proof to show that the weighted geometric mean above is concave on $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$.

Exercise 2.11
Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function. Define the function $g:\operatorname{dom} g\to\mathbf{R}$ by

\[
g(x,t)=f(x/t),
\]

with domain

\[
\operatorname{dom} g=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid x/t\in \operatorname{dom} f,\ t>0\}.
\]

Show that $g$ is quasiconvex.

Exercise 2.12
Continued fraction function. Let $f:D\to\mathbb{R}$ be the function of $x=(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)\in\mathbb{R}^4$ defined by

\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}},
\]

where the domain $D$ consists of all $x\in\mathbb{R}^4$ for which every denominator in the continued fraction is positive, i.e.,
\[
x_4>0,\qquad x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}>0,\qquad x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}>0,\qquad x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}>0.
\]
Show that $f$ is convex on $D$ and decreasing in each variable. (There is nothing special about $n=4$ here; the same holds for any number of variables.)

Exercise 2.13
Circularly symmetric Huber function. Let $n\in\mathbf{N}$ and $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$. The scalar Huber function is defined as

\[
f_{\mathrm{hub}}(t)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)t^2 & |t|\leq 1\\
|t|-1/2 & |t|>1.
\end{cases}
\]

This convex function comes up in several applications, including robust estimation. This problem concerns generalizations of the Huber function to $\mathbf{R}^n$. One generalization to $\mathbf{R}^n$ is given by $f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_1)+\cdots+f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_n)$, but this function is not circularly symmetric, i.e., invariant under transformation of $x$ by an orthogonal matrix. A generalization to $\mathbf{R}^n$ that is circularly symmetric is

\[
f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|_2)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)\|x\|_2^2 & \|x\|_2\leq 1\\
\|x\|_2-1/2 & \|x\|_2>1.
\end{cases}
\]

where $\|\cdot\|_2$ denotes the Euclidean norm. (The subscript stands for `circularly symmetric Huber function'.) Show that $f_{\mathrm{cshub}}:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex. Find its conjugate function $f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*$.

Exercise 2.14
Reverse Jensen inequality. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^m\to\mathbf{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ be a convex function, and suppose $x_1,\ldots,x_n\in\operatorname{dom} f$. Let scalars $\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n$ satisfy $\lambda_1>0$, $\lambda_i\leq 0$ for $i=2,\ldots,n$, and $\lambda_1+\cdots+\lambda_n=1$. Show that

\[
f(\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n)\geq \lambda_1f(x_1)+\cdots+\lambda_nf(x_n).
\]

Hints. Draw a picture for the $n=2$ case first. For the general case, express $x_1$ as a convex combination of $\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n$ and $x_2,\ldots,x_n$, and use Jensen's inequality.

Exercise 2.15
Monotone extension of a convex function. Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex. Recall that a function $h:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is monotone nondecreasing if $h(x)\geq h(y)$ whenever $x\geq y$, where the inequality is understood componentwise. The monotone extension of $f$ is defined as

\[
g(x)=\inf_{z\in\mathbf{R}^n,\ z\geq 0} f(x+z), \qquad x\in\mathbf{R}^n,
\]

where $z\geq 0$ is also understood componentwise. Assume that $g(x)>-\infty$ for all $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$. Show that $g$ is convex and monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies $g(x)\leq f(x)$ for all $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$. Show that if $h:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is any other convex function that satisfies these properties, then $h(x)\leq g(x)$ for all $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$. Thus, $g$ is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of $f$.

Remark. For simple functions (say, on $\mathbf{R}$) it is easy to work out what $g$ is, given $f$. On $\mathbf{R}^n$, it can be very difficult to work out an explicit expression for $g$. However, systems such as CVX can immediately handle functions such as $g$, defined by partial minimization.

Exercise 2.16
\emph{Circularly symmetric convex functions.} Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a convex function that is symmetric with respect to rotations, i.e., for every orthogonal matrix $Q\in \mathbf{R}^{n\times n}$ and every $x\in \mathbf{R}^n$, we have $f(Qx)=f(x)$; equivalently, $f(x)$ depends only on $\|x\|_2$. Show that there exists a function $\phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}$, convex and nondecreasing, such that
\[
f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2), \qquad x\in \mathbf{R}^n,
\]
with $\operatorname{dom}\phi=\mathbf{R}$. (Conversely, any function of this form is symmetric and convex, so this form characterizes such functions.)

Exercise 2.17
\emph{Infimal convolution.} Let $f_1,\ldots,f_m:\mathbf{R}^n\to \mathbf{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ be convex functions. Their infimal convolution, denoted $g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m$ (several other notations are also used), is defined by

\[
g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1,\ldots,x_m\in\mathbf{R}^n,\ x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},
\]

for $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$, with effective domain $\operatorname{dom} g=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid g(x)<+\infty\}$. In one simple interpretation, $f_i(x_i)$ is the cost for the $i$th firm to produce a mix of products given by $x_i$; $g(x)$ is then the optimal cost obtained if the firms can freely exchange products to produce, all together, the mix given by $x$. Exercise 2.30
Huber penalty. Let $f,g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be defined by
\[
f(x)=\|x\|_1, \qquad g(x)=\frac{1}{2}\|x\|_2^2,
\]
where $\|\cdot\|_1$ and $\|\cdot\|_2$ denote the usual $\ell_1$- and Euclidean norms on $\mathbf{R}^n$. The infimal convolution of two functions $f$ and $g$ on $\mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as
\[
h(x)=\inf_{y\in\mathbf{R}^n} \left(f(y)+g(x-y)\right)
\]
(see exercise 2.17). Show that the infimal convolution of $f$ and $g$, i.e., the function
\[
h(x)=\inf_{y\in\mathbf{R}^n} \left(f(y)+g(x-y)\right)
=\inf_{y\in\mathbf{R}^n} \left(\|y\|_1+\frac{1}{2}\|x-y\|_2^2\right),
\]
can be written as the Huber penalty
\[
h(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n \phi(x_i), \qquad
\phi(u)=
\begin{cases}
 u^2/2, & |u|\leq 1,\\
 |u|-1/2, & |u|>1.
\end{cases}
\]
for all $x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbf{R}^n$.
